title: The March Of Ivan
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are you coming, Ivan, coming?, Ah, the ways are long and slow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the vast land that we know not, and we never sought to know.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We are watching through the daybreak, when the anxious night is done,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the dots upon the skyline, black against the rising sun;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We are watching through the morning haze, and waiting through the night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the long, dark, distant columns that proclaim the Muscovite!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are you coming, Ivan, coming? (Oh! the world is growing gray
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With the terror of the future and the madness of to-day!)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are you marching, Ivan, forward? (Oh! the world is darkן¿½ning fast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the crimes of greater nations ן¿½gainst the small ones in the past.)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yours, in part, to make atonement, so remember what you are!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ivan! Sing!, ן¿½The Slav is coming! On for Russia and the Czar!ן¿½


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ivanן¿½s Song

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yes, Iן¿½m coming, Ivan, coming, I am marching out again
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the weary roads of Russia, past the forest, marsh and plain;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Past the field and past the village, in the shine and in the rain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the cart-rut and the grass-track and the jolting cattle-train.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (And, maybe, some gleam of glory penetrates my sluggish brain)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I am marching out for Russia, and for Europe and for you,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, maybe, Iן¿½m mainly marching just because they told me to.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I have marched to many frontiers, in the pregnant days gone by,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When they told us where to march to, but they did not tell us why.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they showed us whom to fight with, and they told us where to die.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I have seen our grey battalions to their Heaven, or Hades, hurled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas enough it was for Russia!, what cared we about the world?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Did one moan of Ivanן¿½s mother penetrate to other lands?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did one prayer of Ivanן¿½s father, with his old and knotted hands?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did one sob from Ivanן¿½s sweetheart, or one cry from Ivanן¿½s wife?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or a wail from Ivanן¿½s children, for the loss of Ivanן¿½s life?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Marching with the Wolf of Hunger, marching with the Bear of Strength!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We have marched for many winters, but the end is near at length!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Tis a long, long march from Plevna, when the Bear went to his den.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It is far from the Crimea; Oh! you did not want us then!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the shambles of Port Arthur, ן¿½twas a weary way and slow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And our track was always dotted with the black dots on the snow.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By black dots and crimson splashes you may trace poor Ivanן¿½s track,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I think that Ivanן¿½s banner should be red, and white and black.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Neן¿½er was Present-blinded tyrant who learnt wisdom from the Past,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thereן¿½s one forgot the errand that brought Ivan this way last!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To the frontier, and no furtherן¿½ seemed our motto and our vow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since we marched from burning Moscow, but weן¿½re marching further now!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Neighbourן¿½s burning house, or city!, they are easy to forget;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But we lit a light for Europe that shall be rekindled yet!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Never song of Ivanן¿½s valour, or of ן¿½Russiaן¿½s Flag Unfurled!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or the Iron Cross of Russia, penetrates the Outer World.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ye! who civilise and peddle, ye who hesitate and lag,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Never heard the Russian March and never saw the Russian flag!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You have called on us to save you, never saying why, or how,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the samovar is boiling! and youן¿½ll hear and see us now.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½From our garrets and our cellars, from the little all we had,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where the winter brings the sleigh-bells to the streets of Petrograd;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From our huts and from our hutches, from wherever we may be,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From our goatן¿½s-flesh and our mareן¿½s milk and our black unsweetened tea,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the silence of Siberia, and the strange, snow-deadened streets,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the blazing banks of Jordan, where we dip our winding-sheets.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From our black bread and our vodka, asking naught, and undismayed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From our never-empty cradles!, we are coming to your aid.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Oh, we leave no bands behind us, blaring tunes of Tyranny,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And we wave no swords above us, yelling songs of Liberty;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And no blatant voice of ruler, as we tramp through dry and wet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Blares: ן¿½Remember You are Russians!ן¿½, weן¿½re not likely to forget.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There are some who have forgotten, merely men, like you and me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And one object of our marching is to jog their memory.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You shall hear us, you shall see us, save the dead and deaf and blind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While the armour of our winter hardens thick and fast behind.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We are marching, we are coming, and we are not on the sea,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You shall see us on the furthest frontier of our enemy!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And while you fix up your frontiers, and remember what you are,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We shall march with Peace for Europe!, back to Russia and the Czar.ן¿½
meaning:
"The March Of Ivan" is a quintessential example of Henry Lawson's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
